fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the scaffolding tool we've been building. The core idea is pretty straightforward — devs give it a template folder and some answers, and it spits out a fully populated project. But there are a bunch of edge cases we keep running into that need to be nailed down before we ship.

First, the file-naming thing — some files should get their contents filled in, others should just be copied as-is, and the folder/file names themselves can also have placeholders. We also need that 'safety valve' mode where you can preview what would happen without actually writing anything to disk.

There's also the question of what happens when you run it again over an existing project — we don't want people losing hand-edited files. The 'protect certain files' logic should work like we did with the login module's compatibility handling, but applied to file paths.

We need config file support too — the template should be able to ship its own defaults, and those settings need to feed into the engine options correctly. The validation layer for flags and template engine settings also needs to be tight — wrong types or conflicting options should surface clean errors, not stack traces.

Finally, there's the external template thing where a child template references a parent that lives somewhere else. If that parent can't be found, or the path doesn't exist at all, we need distinct, clear error messages. Can someone make sure all these pieces are wired up properly?

One more pass on details the team asked about: `.tmpl` is the switch for whether we render file contents. Anything ending in `.tmpl` gets expanded through the templating engine and then the `.tmpl` suffix is removed in the output name. Anything without `.tmpl` is copied verbatim. If the source has both `foo.txt` and `foo.txt.tmpl`, the `.tmpl` one wins and the plain file is ignored. Same placeholder style everywhere too: both file names and directory names can include `[[ name ]]`, and template content uses that exact `[[ name ]]` form for substitution as well. We should resolve those path placeholders from the supplied answers before writing the destination path.

On config merging, the order should be explicit call arguments first, then config-file values, then built-in defaults. So if `include=["aaa"]` is passed as an argument and the config file declares `_include: [include1, include2]`, the final resolved `include` is `["aaa"]`. For the reporting side, the per-entry action labels should be exactly `create`, `identical`, `conflict`, `force`, and `skip`. Folders should show with a trailing `/`, like `create sub/`. The action lines should be sorted, and the output should start with `lines=N`.

For errors going to stdout, keep them language-neutral and structured. Use category codes like `error=invalid_config_data` and `error=template_reference_not_found`, then put the structured fields on following lines. We should never leak exception class names, runtime message text, or object dumps. Also, both `src_path` and `dst_path` are required. If one is missing, report `error=invalid_config_data` with `field=dst_path reason=missing` and/or `field=src_path reason=missing`. If `src_path` is present but the directory is missing, that should be `field=src_path reason=not_found`. If there are multiple field issues, report them together and sort them by field name.

On preview mode, `pretend=true` still needs to run the whole decision process — placeholder expansion, file selection, overwrite resolution, all of it — it just must not write anything to disk. In that mode the output should always say `produced=0` no matter how many files would have been created. Also for the overwrite protection piece, the `skip_if_exists` list in feature3_overwrite.json should render any patterns with the answers first, so something like `[[ name ]]/c.txt` gets resolved before we match it against existing destination files.

And for the validation items: Feature 7 (`check_flags` op) needs strict boolean checking for `pretend`, `quiet`, `force`, `skip`, `cleanup_on_error`, and it also needs to reject `force`+`skip` together. Feature 8 (`check_envops` op) is the one that validates the delimiter strings and the boolean toggles in the Jinja environment options.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: render(src: dict[str, str], data: dict[str, Any], dst_prefill: dict[str, str] | None = None, exclude: list[str] | None = None, include: list[str] | None = None, skip_if_exists: list[str] | None = None, force: bool = False, skip: bool = False, pretend: bool = False, extra_paths: dict[str, str] | None = None) -> RenderResult

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default exclusion list covers: version-control metadata (`.git`, `.git/*`, `.svn`), compiled/cache artifacts (`*.py[co]`, `__pycache__`, `__pycache__/*`), editor backup files (`~*`), macOS metadata (`.DS_Store`), and the configuration file itself (`copier.yml`, `copier.yaml`). These are excluded unless explicitly re-admitted via the `include` list.

trigger_keywords: how to force a default-excluded file through
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An `include` list can re-admit entries that the default exclusion would otherwise drop, either by exact name (e.g. `.svn`) or by shell-style glob pattern. The `include` list can also be declared in the config file under the `_include` key.

The `skip_if_exists` list names files whose pre-existing content must be preserved even when `force=true` is set globally. Patterns in `skip_if_exists` are themselves rendered with the answers before matching — so a pattern like `[[ name ]]/c.txt` is expanded first. A global `skip=true` preserves every pre-existing file. `force=true` and `skip=true` cannot both be true; that is a mutual-exclusion validation error.

A template file may reference an external template via `[% extends "parent.txt" %]`. The engine resolves this against the source tree plus any additional search paths. If the referenced template cannot be found in any path, the error `error=template_reference_not_found` followed by `name=<reference>` is emitted. If a supplied extra search path does not exist on disk, the error `error=invalid_config_data` with `field=extra_paths reason=not_found` is emitted instead.

The engine looks for an optional YAML configuration file named `copier.yml` or `copier.yaml` in the source tree. Keys prefixed with an underscore (e.g. `_exclude`, `_include`, `_skip_if_exists`, `_tasks`) are engine-option keys. All other keys are answer defaults. Values preserve their native YAML types: strings stay strings, integers stay integers, booleans stay booleans, and sequences stay sequences. A malformed YAML file yields `error=invalid_config_file`. Having both `copier.yml` and `copier.yaml` present yields `error=multiple_config_files`.

Each run-mode flag (`pretend`, `quiet`, `force`, `skip`, `cleanup_on_error`) must be a strict boolean. A non-boolean value yields `error=invalid_flags` with `field=<name> reason=value`. Setting both `force=true` and `skip=true` simultaneously yields `error=invalid_flags` with `field=skip reason=mutually_exclusive`.

The `envops` option set configures the Jinja-style delimiter strings (`block_start_string`, `block_end_string`, `variable_start_string`, `variable_end_string`) and boolean toggles (`autoescape`, `keep_trailing_newline`). Boolean fields must be strict booleans (`reason=value` if not); delimiter fields must be strings (`reason=type` if null or non-string). Unknown extra keys in `envops` are accepted and preserved in the output.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'build_config', 'status', 'render', 'read_config', 'check_flags', 'check_envops', 'check_paths'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: those settings need to feed into the engine options correctly
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Config file keys prefixed with `_` (e.g. `_exclude`, `_include`, `_skip_if_exists`, `_tasks`) are engine-option keys loaded via the `read_config` op and merged via `build_config` with explicit call arguments taking highest precedence

context_pointers.vague_reference: distinct, clear error messages for the external template thing
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Two separate error codes: `error=template_reference_not_found` + `name=<ref>` when the parent template file is missing from all search paths, vs `error=invalid_config_data` + `field=extra_paths reason=not_found` when the extra search path directory itself does not exist

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now — could you check the test cases under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` or the PRD in `start.md` for the exact behavior? If it's about error formatting, the rule is always language-neutral category codes with structured fields, never raw exception messages.

feature10_config_merge.json.description: Merging of configuration sources to build the effective settings. The order of precedence is explicit call arguments over configuration-file answers over built-in defaults. Output reports the resolved selection lists and the destination folder name.

feature11_status_report.json.description: The per-entry action report produced while rendering. A fresh destination yields create actions for every produced folder and file. Re-rendering over an existing destination yields an identical action for unchanged files and a conflict action for changed files, followed by a force action when overwriting is forced or a skip action when preserving is requested. Output lists the actions and their targets.

cases.expected_output:
lines=3
create a.txt
create sub/
create sub/b.txt

lines=3
conflict change.txt
force change.txt
identical keep.txt

lines=3
conflict change.txt
skip change.txt
identical keep.txt

feature1_render_engine.json.description: Rendering of a source template tree into a destination. Files whose name carries the template suffix have their contents expanded with the templating engine using the supplied named answers, and the suffix is stripped from the produced name. Files without the suffix are copied verbatim. Names of files and directories may themselves contain placeholders that are expanded into the produced path. When a plain file and a same-named file carrying the template suffix both exist, the suffixed (rendered) one takes precedence and the plain one is dropped. Output reports the count of produced files, the sorted produced paths, and the produced content of each file.

cases.expected_output:
produced=2
path: README.txt
path: greeting.txt
file: README.txt -> "Static line.\n"
file: greeting.txt -> "Hello world!\n"

produced=2
path: NOTES.txt
path: acme/__init__.py
file: NOTES.txt -> "verbatim content\n"
file: acme/__init__.py -> "name = 'acme'\n"

produced=1
path: README.txt
file: README.txt -> "Rendered for Demo.\n"

feature2_exclude_include.json.description: Selection of which template entries reach the destination. A built-in set of patterns is excluded by default (version-control metadata, compiled/cache artifacts, editor backups, the configuration file itself). An explicit exclude option drops entries matching shell-style patterns. An include option re-admits entries that the default exclusion would otherwise drop, by exact name or by pattern. The configuration file may also declare exclusion and inclusion pattern lists that drive the same selection.

cases.expected_output:
produced=2
path: .svn
path: keep.txt
file: .svn -> "svn\n"
file: keep.txt -> "keep\n"

feature3_overwrite.json.description: Behaviour when a produced file already exists in the destination. A skip-if-exists list (whose patterns are themselves rendered with the answers) preserves the pre-existing content of matching files even when overwriting is forced. A global skip mode preserves every pre-existing file, while a global force mode overwrites every pre-existing file. Output reports the final destination contents.

cases.expected_output:
produced=3
path: a.txt
path: b.txt
path: meh/c.txt
file: a.txt -> "OVERWRITTEN\n"
file: b.txt -> "SKIPPED\n"
file: meh/c.txt -> "SKIPPED\n"

feature4_pretend.json.description: A dry-run mode that performs the full rendering computation but makes no changes to the destination. Output reports that nothing was produced.

feature5_extra_paths.json.description: Resolution of template references that live outside the source tree. Additional search paths may be supplied so that a template can extend or include a shared parent template found there. If a referenced parent template cannot be located in any search path, a template-reference error is reported. If a supplied additional search path does not exist, a configuration-data error is reported categorising the offending field as not found.

feature6_config_loading.json.description: Loading of the optional configuration file from the source tree. Engine-option keys (those carrying a leading-underscore marker) and answer-default keys are both returned, each preserving its native declared type: text stays text, integers stay integers, booleans stay booleans, and sequences stay sequences. A malformed configuration file is reported as an invalid-config-file error. Two configuration files present at once are reported as a multiple-config-files error. When no configuration file is present, no keys are returned.

config_files.name: copier.yml
config_files.content:
a_string: lorem ipsum
a_number: 12345
a_boolean: true
a_list:
  - one
  - two
  - three

cases.expected_output:
keys=4
a_boolean=true
a_list=["one", "two", "three"]
a_number=12345
a_string="lorem ipsum"

feature7_flags_validation.json.description: Validation of the run-mode option set. Each mode is a strict boolean; a non-boolean value is reported as a validation error on the offending field. The overwrite-everything and preserve-everything modes are mutually exclusive and enabling both is reported as a mutual-exclusion error. Unknown extra keys are ignored. Valid input echoes the resolved mode values.

cases.expected_output:
valid=true
cleanup_on_error=true
force=true
pretend=true
quiet=false
skip=false

feature8_envops_validation.json.description: Validation of the templating-engine option set that configures the placeholder and block delimiters and toggles. Boolean toggles must be strict booleans and delimiter strings must be text; violations are reported as a validation error on the offending field. Unknown extra keys are accepted and preserved. Valid input echoes the resolved option values.

cases.expected_output:
valid=true
autoescape=true
block_end_string="%>"
block_start_string="<%"
extra_member=null
keep_trailing_newline=false
variable_end_string=">>"
variable_start_string="<<"

feature9_config_data_validation.json.description: Validation of the resolved configuration data. The source and destination locations are mandatory; omitting them is reported as missing-field errors. A source location (or extra search location) that does not exist is reported as a not-found error. Valid input echoes requested resolved fields, including the destination folder name.
